result:
I’ve been thinking about the way light behaves when it’s almost gone—how it clings to the edges of things like it’s reluctant to leave. Twilight is the shyest hour, slipping in after the loud certainties of noon and before the self-assured dark. It apologizes for both of them, offers a truce: you don’t have to squint, you don’t have to turn on the lamp just yet. For a few minutes the world is upholstered in bruised gold, and every chimney, every cracked window, every questionable life choice you ever made is suddenly forgivable under that palette.

I like to walk then. The streets feel borrowed, like the city has been left in my coat pocket and I’ve forgotten to give it back. Dogs sniff me as if I’m the visitor. A woman on a balcony waters geraniums that have no business blooming this late in October; the water falls in a thin silver rope and disappears into the pavement before it reaches me. I nod, she doesn’t nod back, and somehow that is the most honest conversation I’ll have all week.

There’s a small bakery I aim for, not because the pastries are exceptional—they’re decent, no more—but because the owner keeps a radio behind the counter permanently tuned to a station that plays only forgotten ballads from 1932 to 1941. The songs crackle like cinnamon in hot oil. One night he told me the dial is broken; the station chose him, not the other way around. I think about that a lot. How some frequencies trap you, how you discover you’ve been singing along in a language you never bothered to learn.

Inside, I order whatever is left. He gives it to me in a paper bag soft as old linen, and I carry it like contraband to the river. The water is black velvet by then; even the swans have clocked off. I sit on the concrete lip, legs dangling above the hypothetical tide, and eat the pastry flake by flake. Each mouthful tastes of anonymity, which is surprisingly sweet—like custard left too long under a heat lamp, but still custard. Somewhere behind me a tram brakes, showering blue sparks onto the road. They look like miniature galaxies dying before they hit the ground, and I remember that astronomers say most of the calcium in our bones was cooked inside stars that never got to be anything else. I lick a bit of sugar off my thumb and feel aristocratic: heir to an unfinished cosmos.

When the bag is empty I fold it into a paper boat, christen it with someone else’s name, and launch it. It sinks immediately, of course, but for a moment it floated on the darkness like a promise that forgot its own wording. That is enough. I stand up, knees creaking the way polite floorboards do when they don’t want you to know they’re judging your weight. The city’s lights have multiplied; they shimmer on the water like a second, faker city that believes in itself a little too much. I wave goodbye to it just in case it’s waving back.

Walking home, I try to recall what I worried about this morning. The list has evaporated, leaving only a faint odor of metallic dread, like a coin pressed too long in a sweaty palm. I decide this is progress. Somewhere a cat is screaming romance into the void; somewhere else a refrigerator clicks off, relieved that its shift is over. I pass the bakery again—the radio is playing a song whose title, if translated, would be “We Kissed Until the War Came Back.” I catch myself humming the melody, realize I am now part of the transmission, a small vibrating string in the endless circuitry of night.

At my building the stairwell smells of burnt toast and violin rosin. I climb slowly, counting every third step the way I did when I was small and convinced that counting was a spell against disappearance. On the landing I pause. The bulb overhead flickers like it’s negotiating its own extinction. In that stutter I see, for less than a second, the outline of every future I will not live: the one where I learn to sail, the one where I forgive my father, the one where I never leave this neighborhood and grow famous for the roses I coax out of impossible soil. They layer over each other like acetate sheets, luminous and already tearing at the edges. Then darkness again, thick as custard left under a heat lamp, but still custard.

Inside the apartment I don’t turn on the lights. I drink water from the measuring cup, because the glasses are all somewhere else. The radiator ticks like an old dog dreaming of running. I sit on the floor with my back against the door and listen to the building decide what kind of silence it wants to be. Eventually I will get cold, find the blanket, check my phone, remember the world is still rehearsing its catastrophes. But not yet. For now I am the hour’s custodian, keeper of the almost-gone light, the unpaid guardian of every paper boat that never made it to sea. Outside, the city rearranges itself one brick at a time, and the stars—those ancient calcium factories—keep burning their quiet recipes into our blood. I breathe in, breathe out, feel the twilight settle into my bones like a promise that remembers its wording but refuses to tell me yet.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
